title_slide: Pandas for Data Analysis: 4 Skills for Every Project

slides.heading: The Problem With Most Pandas Tutorials
bullet_points: They teach syntax, not mental models
Real datasets aren't clean CSVs
You need tools for mess, not textbook examples
slides.speaker_note: Open with the uncomfortable truth about what real data work actually looks like.

slides.heading: The Mental Model That Changes Everything
bullet_points: A DataFrame is a structured, labeled, query-able object
Columns are NumPy arrays with names
Operations feel inevitable once you understand this
slides.speaker_note: Explain the foundation before showing any code.

slides.heading: Skill 1: Explore Any Dataset in 60 Seconds
bullet_points: df.shape, df.dtypes, df.head()
df.describe() reveals outliers and skew instantly
df.isnull().sum() — never skip this step
slides.speaker_note: Walk through the exact sequence to run when opening an unfamiliar dataset.

slides.heading: Skill 2: Select and Filter Without the Common Traps
bullet_points: Use & and | not 'and'/'or' — wrap each condition
Switch to .query() at 3+ conditions
Use .isin() instead of stacked OR conditions
slides.speaker_note: These are the mistakes that trip everyone up exactly once.

slides.heading: Skill 3: Clean Messy Data Without Losing Rows
bullet_points: errors='coerce' turns bad values into NaN, not errors
Strip strings: .str.strip().str.title()
Re-run dtypes and isnull() after every cleaning step
slides.speaker_note: Emphasize that cleaning is analysis, not preparation for analysis.

slides.heading: Skill 4: GroupBy and Aggregation
bullet_points: Split → Apply → Combine is the mental model
Use .agg() with named aggregations for clean output
Always reset_index() to return to a flat DataFrame
slides.speaker_note: Show the named aggregation pattern — it's the most underused clean syntax in Pandas.

slides.heading: GroupBy vs Pivot Table
bullet_points: GroupBy: for computation and downstream processing
Pivot table: for communication with stakeholders
Same underlying data, different output shape
slides.speaker_note: This distinction resolves a lot of confusion about when to use each.

slides.heading: What to Do Next
bullet_points: Open a dataset you've been avoiding
Run through all four skills in sequence
Tutorial 5: Visualization — making the data visible
slides.speaker_note: End with a concrete action, not a summary.
